entry.title: Emergent Tool Use in Large Language Models via Reinforcement Learning from Structured Feedback
entry.summary: We present a study on emergent tool-use behaviors in large language models trained with reinforcement learning from structured human feedback. Our findings demonstrate that models above 13B parameters develop consistent tool-invocation strategies without explicit tool-use supervision. We evaluate across 12 benchmark tasks and report a 34% improvement over instruction-tuned baselines on multi-step reasoning tasks.

entry.title: Efficient Context Compression for Long-Document LLM Inference
entry.summary: Context window utilization remains a key bottleneck for long-document tasks in transformer-based language models. We propose a hierarchical compression scheme that reduces effective token count by 60% while preserving 94% of downstream task accuracy on summarization and question-answering benchmarks. Our method requires no fine-tuning and is compatible with any attention mechanism.

entry.title: Multi-Agent Debate as a Calibration Mechanism for Factual Accuracy
entry.summary: We investigate whether structured debate between multiple LLM instances improves factual accuracy on knowledge-intensive tasks. In a series of controlled experiments, we find that two-agent debate with a moderator agent reduces hallucination rate by 41% on medical and legal benchmarks, at the cost of a 3x increase in token expenditure. We analyze failure modes and propose a selective debate triggering heuristic.
